title: How to Contact Police or Get Help in China as a Tourist
metaDescription: Learn how to contact police and get emergency help in China as a tourist. Essential numbers, tips, and Chinese phrases for a safe trip.

sections.heading: Essential Emergency Numbers in China
sections.content: China's universal emergency number is **110** for police, **119** for fire, and **120** for ambulance. These calls are free from any phone. In major cities like Beijing, Shanghai, and Guangzhou, English-speaking operators are often available, especially during daytime hours. Save these numbers in your phone immediately upon arrival. For non-emergency tourist assistance, dial **12345**, a government hotline with multilingual support in many cities.

sections.heading: What to Say When You Call 110
sections.content: Stay calm and speak slowly. State clearly: "I am a tourist. I need help. My location is..." Provide landmarks, hotel names, or nearby subway stations. If you don't speak Mandarin, say "English, please" — dispatchers can often connect you to an English speaker or translator. Keep your phone on and stay visible. Police in tourist areas typically respond within 10–15 minutes. Write down or screenshot your hotel address in Chinese characters to show officers easily.

sections.heading: Visiting a Police Station (派出所)
sections.content: You can walk into any local police station (派出所, pàichūsuǒ) to report theft, lost items, or harassment. Bring your passport — it's required for any official report. Officers may use translation apps to communicate with you. Ask for a written report copy, as you may need it for insurance claims. Police stations in tourist districts often have dedicated foreign affairs desks. Useful phrase: "我要报案" (Wǒ yào bào'àn) — "I want to file a report."

sections.heading: Using Technology to Get Help Fast
sections.content: Download WeChat before your trip — many police stations and hotels use it for quick communication. Enable location sharing with trusted contacts. Apps like Baidu Maps and Apple Maps work well in China and can pinpoint your exact location for responders. The **110 WeChat mini-program** in some cities lets you send text-based emergency reports with GPS coordinates. Keep a screenshot of your hotel's Chinese address and your emergency contact's phone number accessible offline.

sections.heading: Medical Emergencies and Hospitals
sections.content: For medical emergencies, call **120** for an ambulance or go directly to a hospital's emergency room (急诊, jízhěn). International hospitals and VIP clinics in major cities have English-speaking staff. Always carry travel insurance documentation. Useful phrase: "我需要看医生" (Wǒ xūyào kàn yīshēng) — "I need to see a doctor." Bring your passport and insurance card. Note: This article provides general guidance only and is not a substitute for professional medical or legal advice. Consult your embassy for legal assistance.

sections.heading: Extra Safety Tips for Tourists
sections.content: Register with your country's embassy upon arrival for emergency notifications. Keep digital and physical copies of your passport, visa, and insurance. China is generally very safe for tourists, with low violent crime rates. Petty theft can occur in crowded tourist areas — stay aware of your belongings. If you lose your passport, contact your embassy immediately and file a police report. Most Chinese people are incredibly helpful and will go out of their way to assist a tourist in need.

keyTakeaways: Call 110 for police emergencies — it's free and available 24/7 from any phone in China
Always carry your passport; it's required when filing any police report
Save your hotel's address in Chinese characters as a screenshot on your phone
Major cities have English-speaking operators and foreign affairs police desks
Download WeChat and offline maps before your trip for faster emergency communication
relatedPhrases: 我要报案 (Wǒ yào bào'àn) — I want to file a report
请帮我 (Qǐng bāng wǒ) — Please help me
我迷路了 (Wǒ mílù le) — I am lost
我的护照丢了 (Wǒ de hùzhào diūle) — I lost my passport
请叫救护车 (Qǐng jiào jiùhùchē) — Please call an ambulance
